candidate_problems.text: Can 'record entropy' be defined as a precise, observer-independent physical quantity that is not just a relabeling of coarse-grained thermodynamic entropy? The conjecture hinges on low-record boundary conditions doing explanatory work beyond standard low-entropy accounts, but it remains unclear how to formalize records, durability, copyability, and asymmetry in a way that yields distinct predictions.

By what concrete dynamical mechanism do time-symmetric micro-laws plus a low-record boundary generically produce monotonic growth of durable, usable records rather than merely transient correlations? The open question is whether record accumulation, error-correction, and selection-like persistence truly follow for broad classes of systems, or only under additional hidden assumptions about locality, decoherence, coarse-graining, or available free energy.

Why should the universe have a one-sided informationally sparse boundary at all, instead of two low-record boundaries, no such boundary, or a symmetric condition? The conjecture relocates the explanatory burden to ontology, but leaves unresolved what deeper principle, law, or cosmological mechanism selects an informationally simple boundary and thereby fixes the arrow relevant to knowledge.
